def: A namespace attached to a Series that exposes operations specific to its content type. .str exposes string methods (strip, lower); .dt exposes datetime methods (year, month); .cat exposes categorical operations. Methods called through an accessor are applied element-wise across the Series.

A short name bound to a module by the as clause of an import. The conventions import pandas as pd, import numpy as np, import matplotlib.pyplot as plt are followed almost universally and should be retained.

An application programming interface — a defined contract by which one piece of software requests data or actions from another. In data work, frequently used to describe an HTTP-based service that returns JSON in response to a query.

A value passed into a function call. Positional arguments are matched by position (the file path in pd.read_csv("file.csv")); keyword arguments are matched by name (sep=','). The function's signature determines which positional arguments are required and which keyword arguments accept defaults.

A statement that raises an exception if a stated condition is false. Used as a self-check at the boundary between steps — for instance assert df.shape[0] == 320 both documents and enforces the expected row count.
A value attached to an object, accessed without parentheses: df.shape. Attributes describe state; methods perform actions.

In Matplotlib, the coordinate system on which a single plot is drawn. A figure may contain one axes (single plot) or several (subplots). Plotting calls (ax.bar, ax.plot, …) are issued on an axes object rather than on the figure itself.

The dimension along which an operation is performed. axis=0 moves down rows (column-wise reduction); axis=1 moves across columns (row-wise reduction). For concatenation, the axis is the dimension that grows.

An argument to plt.savefig controlling the bounding box of the saved figure. bbox_inches='tight' crops surrounding whitespace so that axis labels, titles, and legends are not clipped at the image edge.
A value that is either True or False. Comparison operators (==, <, >=) produce booleans; pandas operations on whole columns produce boolean Series, which can in turn be used to filter rows.

The rule by which NumPy and pandas perform arithmetic between arrays of different but compatible shapes. A scalar combines with every element; a shorter array is virtually repeated to match a longer one.

In a join, the number of rows on one side that correspond to a row on the other. Common cardinalities: one-to-one (each key appears at most once on either side), one-to-many (one row on the left corresponds to several on the right), many-to-one (several rows on the left share one row on the right). validate="m:1" asserts the expected cardinality.

A sequence of method calls applied to successive results: df.dropna().sort_values('x').head(10). Chaining keeps an intermediate-free style; each pandas method returns a new DataFrame, which is the next method's input.
A vertical slice of a DataFrame — one variable, one type. Accessed as df['col'].

A concise expression that builds a list (or other collection) by iterating over an iterable: [x**2 for x in range(10)]. May include a condition: [x for x in xs if x > 0]. Equivalent to a for-loop that appends, but more readable and slightly faster.

Indexing operations sometimes return a view (a window into the original) and sometimes a copy. Assigning to a view modifies the original; assigning to a copy does not. The safe pattern for writing back is df.loc[rows, col] = value.
A frequency table of two categorical variables. pd.crosstab(df['ward'], df['gender']) returns a table whose rows are wards, columns are genders, and cells are counts.
A two-dimensional, labelled table — the central pandas object. Conceptually a dictionary of columns, each column a Series sharing a common index.
The character that separates fields in a delimited text file. Comma in classical CSV, semicolon in many European exports, tab in TSV. Set explicitly via sep= when not the default comma.
A Python collection storing values under named keys: {"M": "male", "F": "female"}. Used as the mapping argument to replace, rename, agg, and many other pandas methods.
Dots per inch — the resolution of a raster image. dpi=200 in plt.savefig produces a sufficiently sharp image for slides; dpi=300 is the typical print standard.
The data type of a column or Series — for example int64, float64, object (typically strings), category, datetime64. The dtype determines which operations are permitted.
The mapping between bytes on disk and characters in memory. UTF-8 covers most modern text; older Windows exports often use cp1252 or latin-1.

A signal that something went wrong during execution. Pandas and NumPy raise specific exceptions (KeyError, ValueError, MergeError) that name the cause. Handled with try / except blocks where appropriate; otherwise read the message and the traceback for the source line.

The width and height of a Matplotlib figure in inches, passed as a tuple to plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6)). Determines aspect ratio and reading comfort; (10, 6) is a sensible default for slide-ready figures.
In Matplotlib, the outermost container — the canvas on which one or more axes are drawn. The figure is the object that plt.savefig writes; the axes are what individual plot calls draw on.
A named, reusable block of code. Functions take inputs (arguments), perform a computation, and may return an output. Defined with def name(args): ....
The operation of partitioning a DataFrame by the values of one or more key columns and applying a function within each partition. The split–apply–combine model.

The first row of a CSV file when it contains the column names. pd.read_csv treats row 0 as the header by default; header=None indicates that no header row is present, in which case columns may be named via names=[...].
The statement that loads a module into the current namespace. import pandas as pd binds the pandas library to the alias pd.

The replacement of a missing value (NaN) by an estimated substitute — most often a central value of the column (median, mean) or a domain default. Imputation makes the decision visible; allowing NaN to propagate hides it inside downstream calculations.

An operation that modifies an object directly rather than returning a new one. Many pandas methods accept an inplace=True argument, though the idiomatic style is to reassign the return value: df = df.dropna().
The labels assigned to the rows (or, for columns, to the columns) of a DataFrame. By default a RangeIndex 0…n−1, but may be set to any meaningful key (date, identifier).
A join that retains only rows whose key is present in both tables.

A widely used text format for structured data: nested combinations of objects (key-value pairs), arrays, strings, numbers, booleans, and null. The default response format of most HTTP APIs. Read with pd.read_json or parsed with Python's built-in json module.
The column whose values determine which row of one table corresponds to which row of another in a merge.
An argument passed to a function by name: encoding='utf-8'. Keyword arguments may appear in any order after the positional arguments and typically have defaults that may be omitted.
A small, unnamed function defined inline: lambda x: x.upper(). Used where a one-line function is needed for a single call, as in df['x'].apply(lambda v: v.strip()).
A join that retains every row of the left (calling) table, filling missing matches from the right table with NaN.
A boolean array (or Series) used to select rows. df[mask] returns the rows where mask is True.
The arithmetic average — the sum of values divided by the count. Sensitive to outliers: a single extreme value can shift the mean substantially. Compare with the median, which is robust.

The middle value when the column is sorted — half the values lie below it, half above. Robust to outliers: unlike the mean, it is not pulled by extreme values. Preferred over the mean for skewed distributions.
A function attached to an object, called with dot notation: df.head(). The object on which it is called provides part of the input.

A mutable object can be changed in place (lists, dictionaries, DataFrames). An immutable object cannot (tuples, strings, integers). Mutability matters because mutable defaults in function definitions are shared across calls.
«Not a Number» — the marker pandas uses for missing values. NaN is never equal to anything, including itself; missingness is tested with isna(), not == NaN.
The missing-value marker for datetime columns — the temporal analogue of NaN.

To bring values into a single, consistent form. In data cleaning: collapsing alternate spellings of a category to a canonical label. In statistics: rescaling values to a common range, such as 0–1 or zero-mean unit-variance.
A join that retains every row from both tables, filling missing matches with NaN. Useful for auditing which keys are present in only one source.

A value that lies far from the bulk of the data. May indicate a measurement error, an input error, or a genuine rare event. Outliers disproportionately influence the mean, the range, and least-squares fits; the median and the quantiles are robust to them.
A named input to a function. Positional arguments are matched by position; keyword arguments by name (sep=',').
The location of a file in the filesystem. Constructed with pathlib.Path for portability across operating systems.
Python's package installer. pip install pandas installs the pandas library into the active Python environment.
A reshaping that turns long-format data into wide format: distinct values of one variable become the column labels of the result.

To let a value travel through subsequent computations unchanged. With respect to NaN: a propagating operation returns NaN wherever an input was NaN. Pandas's mean, sum, and groupby do not propagate NaN by default — they skip it. The option skipna=False forces propagation, useful when the presence of any missingness should make the result itself missing.

Produced by a deterministic algorithm that, given a starting state (the seed), generates a sequence resembling randomness. Reproducible: the same seed yields the same sequence. Necessary for teaching examples, statistical simulations, and any analysis whose results must be verifiable.
Quartiles divide an ordered distribution into four equal parts: Q1 (25th percentile), Q2 (median), Q3 (75th percentile). The interquartile range (IQR) is Q3 − Q1, a robust measure of spread.
An image stored as a grid of pixels; quality depends on resolution. PNG and JPG are raster formats. In contrast, vector formats (PDF, SVG) store geometric instructions and remain sharp at any size.

To replace a value or category with another that better serves the analysis — for instance, mapping NaN to the explicit category "Unknown", or collapsing several specific diagnoses into a broader class.

A pandas method that renumbers the rows to a clean 0…n-1 sequence. After filtering or dropping rows, the index has gaps; reset_index(drop=True) restores consecutive numbering and discards the old index instead of retaining it as an additional column.
A horizontal slice of a DataFrame — one observation across all variables. Accessed by position with df.iloc[i] or by label with df.loc[label].

A single value, as opposed to an array or Series. A number, a string, a boolean — anything that is not a collection. Most NumPy and pandas operations broadcast scalars across the whole array or column.

A special, agreed-upon value used by a source system to signal "no valid value here" — typically because the export format cannot store NaN directly. Common conventions: -1, -99, 9999, the string "N/A". Sentinel values must be translated to NaN before analysis; otherwise they are treated as genuine measurements and distort every subsequent statistic.
A one-dimensional, labelled array — a single column of a DataFrame, or a standalone 1D structure. Carries both values and an index.
The dimensions of an array or DataFrame as a tuple: (rows, columns) for a 2D structure, (n,) for a 1D Series or array. Accessed as the .shape attribute, without parentheses.

An argument to many pandas aggregations controlling whether NaN values are excluded from the calculation. Default is True — NaN is ignored. skipna=False forces NaN to propagate, so the result is NaN whenever any input was NaN.
A selection of a subrange of a sequence or array using start:stop:step notation. arr[2:7] takes elements 2 through 6; df.iloc[:, 1:4] takes columns 1 through 3 of every row.
The conceptual model of groupby: split the table by a key, apply a function to each group, combine the results into a single summary.
One of several axes arranged in a grid within a single figure. Created with fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3) for a 2×3 grid; each cell is then drawn on with axes[i, j].
A portion of a DataFrame — a selection of certain rows, columns, or both. Used as an argument name in pandas (e.g. dropna(subset=['col']) restricts the operation to specified columns).
A convention in which each variable forms a column, each observation forms a row, and each type of observational unit forms a table. Pandas operations are most natural on tidy data.
A Matplotlib function that recomputes the spacing between axes elements so that titles, labels, and tick marks do not overlap. Called immediately before savefig or show.

The chain of function calls that led to an exception, printed when an error occurs. Read from bottom upward: the last line names the exception type and its message; the lines above show the path through which the error was reached. The deepest entry inside the user's own code is usually where to start investigating.

An ordered, immutable Python sequence, written with parentheses: (1, 2, 3). Used for fixed-length records where the position of each element is meaningful — for instance, the .shape of a DataFrame is a tuple.

A keyword argument to pd.merge that asserts the expected cardinality of the join ('1:1', '1:m', 'm:1', or 'm:m') and raises an exception if the assumption is violated. Recommended on any non-trivial merge as protection against silently inflated results.
The application of an operation to a whole array or column in a single step, dispatched to compiled code. Markedly faster than the equivalent Python loop.
Characters that appear blank: ordinary spaces, tabs, and line breaks. Often present at the start or end of strings from manual data entry; removed by .str.strip() before grouping or comparison.
